text:
This is really really cool and super close to what I'm working on right now. I actually have a useful question for whoever has an idea: I can't share the exact details, but I"m building an examinator/tutor simulator for a certain oral test. There's a constant set of questions with sets of constant answer variants for each question. I'm building the voice system that will ask a question and listen to the answer, my server logic adjudicates correctness based on a couple of matching algorithms, then comments or starts a short side conversation based on the answer and voice telemetry. My planned stack was almost exactly as I see in the video: Deepgram STT - LLM (I want to use Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Preview) - TTS (most likely Inworld for a good selection of voices). LLM here is only for occasional comments (some snarky jokes, or words of encouragement), occasional hints if the users's answer is 80% close to correct or additional layer of adjudication (if the answer grade is borderline). This system doesn't require Websockets and the adjudication can stay deterministic server side (this kind of ensures simplicity and easier maintenance and debugging)
.
But I've been impressed by this Gemini Live model and thinking to try it as well. But after consulting with other LLM models about the architecture flow, I"m still recommended to stick to my design to keep adjudication very deterministic and not fully dependant on LLM making all the interview decisions. But what I see in this video is very intriguing.

I'm mostly reading forums and consulting with friends who haven't built anything like that, so I'm willing to stick to my plan as my dataset of questions and answers is constant for the next few years. But this videos shows a smooth conversation. I'm definitely going with Google Gemini as it fits my budget, but if someone's built something similar with their models, I'd appreciate your feedback. Especially: is it more expensive to have Gemini Live run the conversation and call the tools than STT - LLM -TTS pipeline? What if the conversation lasts around 20 minutes? (Like the food ordering feature in this video: can you somehow ensure, that Gemini doesn't make mistakes when picking the tool call? Or it can't be deterministic by the nature of neural nets?)
